## Curious

# The Complex Interculturality of World Literature

![Yu-Chan Moriguchi](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*PoK8f7CC9Mf9Ti9Kksmfuw.jpeg)

--

Listen

Share

![]()

The importance of interculturality is promoting awareness of the attitudes, values and beliefs of different cultures in order to create a society that is compassionate, perceptive and righteous. It is a dynamic process that leads to a greater understanding of our place in the world and the shared humanity that is present across cultures. It can emerge through many forms but perhaps the greatest and most profound medium for interculturality is the art form of World Literature. It not only serves as a vessel through which authors can illustrate the many stories that define a cultural identity but also demonstrate the complexity and diversity present in our vast world.

The impact that World Literature can have on an entire generation has never before been more evident than in the 21st century. In an article from Dhaka Tribune titled, [“Bringing the world into the classroom through world literature”](https://www.dhakatribune.com/magazine/arts-letters/2018/07/14/bringing-the-world-into-the-classroom-through-world-literature), Rita Nezami recounts the extraordinary influence that World Literature had on shaping the perspectives of her students. While previously there was an abstract and single-minded understanding of global issues that had seemingly no value or importance to them, what now emerged were students who had experienced the joys and sufferings of others as though it was their own. The novels they read featured characters from cultures who they previously never fully understood but now felt like individuals from places that were tangible, authentic and beautiful in their unique ways. The writers of these global literary texts offered “a vision of a common humanity, of a common pain, of a common oppression, and, so often, of a common poverty”.

![]()

The compassionate and sympathetic perspective attained by these students is something that I adamantly strive for. Therefore, over the course of the past few weeks, I have been on a journey through the World Cup of Literature where I have been exposed to a range of diverse and challenging literature from around the globe. The aim of this challenge was to develop a more authentic and personal understanding of the complex interrelationships between and within cultures, ultimately leading to a broader and more holistic view of our interconnected world. There were several enlightening and enriching stories yet no story had quite the profound and emotional impact that Latha accomplished in her sweeping and intimate tale known as [*There Was a Bridge in Tekka*](https://www.wordswithoutborders.org/article/october-2016-multilingual-singapore-there-was-a-bridge-in-tekka-latha)from Singapore*.*

In this monumental and moving tale set in Singapore, an Indian woman named Latha perseveres through the struggles of cultural conflicts while also reflecting on the nature of forgotten memories. Set predominantly in the backdrop of the 1940s, this is a time when Singapore is under Japanese occupation. The importing of foreign material and overseas letters is suspended which leaves only the spoken word as a vessel through which the public can stay informed of current affairs. The major turning point in the narrative occurs when Latha and her best friend, Akka, become swept up in a revolution led by Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose to free India from the rule of the British. In a fiery passion, Netaji exclaims, “Give us your blood, and in return, I will give you your freedom!”.

![]()

It becomes clear that the historical context surrounding this visceral story is smeared by the effects of interrelationships and that the cultural practices of the Indian immigrant community have become suppressed by the power of other nations. The hope that permeates throughout the audience as the great Chandra Bose promises that “Should India gain independence, we can all return to India” is a feeling that is almost alien to me. Living freely in Australia ever since I was born, I have never had to deal with great oppression of my cultural identity. I have never had to fight for my freedom of expression or freedom of identity.

The nature of the story is intrinsically reflective of intercultural relations because despite originating from Singapore, the story is focused on the Indian immigrant community within that country. This unique approach is why such a story was so enthralling and fascinating. The intimate and personal touches help to craft an immersive experience where I felt both the joys and sadness of Latha’s life in an authentic style that another medium would never have been able to accurately capture. I was enlightened to the destructive effects of war and realised how privileged I was to live in a free and diverse society, compared to the reality that faces Latha.

![]()

What Latha ultimately achieved in her story was a nuanced perspective on the complexity of interculturality. The world of her story is multicultural but the intercultural relations that have occurred have had a damaging effect on the Indian community. In their struggle for freedom, lives are lost yet the memories of these important events have been forgotten as time has gone on. A sense of whimsical nostalgia and bitter heartbreak is deeply felt throughout the course of the entire narrative. This balance between wonder and misery is felt most profoundly when we learn that Latha’s best friend, Akka, only discovered her hometown on the day that she succumbed to death. This tragic outcome has had its roots planted generations before yet it occurs after possibly the most authentic cultural moment of Akka’s entire life when she visits the burial place of her father and husband at a temple in her hometown.

What I gained most from this tale was a broader understanding of how interculturality can be both wonderful and detrimental. Diversity is important but when cultural identity is suppressed, it can lead to the corruption of cultural interrelationships with irreversible consequences. Latha uses the power of world literature to encapsulate an entire world of experiences and to remind the audience of a past that should never be forgotten. The river may be buried and the bridge is no more, but the stories will always live on as long as there is someone there to tell them.

![]()

After being taken on quite the epic and invigorating experience, it seemed as though World Literature was a great force of nature. I soon realised though that within the powerful veil lay a tumultuous and questionable journey in the circulation of its stories. To discover the stories that define a culture, they must be translated across the language barrier in order to gain a wider audience. However, this has created unintentional challenges for truly living in an interconnected and culturally diverse world. Translation can warp words, alter meanings and unravel the creative intentions of the author. At the same time though, it can also allow for stories to evolve and expand their meaning beyond what was originally envisioned.

In this Singaporean poem, [*The Un-God*](https://www.poetrytranslation.org/poems/the-un-god/notes), also written by Latha, cultural and societal burdens are depicted in an abstract, unconventional manner. This succinct, concise poem about the abortion of a child leans heavily into Tamil culture. However, the poem is intentionally vague in the reasoning behind this abortion which allows for it to resonate broadly due to the universality of its themes. It seems to flow from a beautiful description of a ritual as evident by the “Tumeric and pale-scented jasmine on the vine” and the “Raw-green tumeric-yellow sky-blue violet-inked butterflies” which evoke a sense of wonder and awe at the allure of life, to a cold and unsettling sense of tragedy as the mother tells her unborn child, “Yes, you will never become a god”, cementing the loss of the un-god.

![]()

In the circulation of this poem to English, translation has played a critical role in allowing the story to evolve both in its meaning and its emotional weight. The authentic and raw nature of this translation is the result of artistic license as to simply write a literal translation would lose much of the nuance that is fundamental to the art of poetry. However, the evolution of the poem has allowed it to continue to maintain relevance and meaning beyond its origins. Through this artistic expression, a sense of beauty and tragedy is built which causes the readers to develop sympathy and understanding for something that they may never have realised was so deeply meaningful to an entire demographic. Poetry is the art that lives on and translation is the fuel to its survival across the world.

Yet many people would believe it to be sacrilegious to translate a text because it leads to the damaging of intercultural relations much like how the Indian immigrant community was suppressed in *There Was a Bridge in Tekka.* This is the inherent complexity of world literature. It both seems to promote interculturality while also possibly being damaging to the artistry and diversity of cultures. This a fine line that is walked by World Literature and it is hard to determine whether it leans more to one side or the other.

![]()

From both Latha’s short story and poem, there is so much beauty and understanding of global issues and cultural conflicts, yet at the same time, there is a questionable nature in the construction of the translations that lingers heavily in my mind. World Literature is an art form like any other and will constantly be subject to criticism and praise. Nonetheless, it is worth exploring because both the positive and negative insights gained can enlighten us to be better people who are more appreciative of the lives that we live. It contextualises our struggles on a global scale, ultimately opening us up to greater compassion and greater humanity.

**Bibliography**

Bringing the world into the classroom through world literature (2018). Available at: <https://www.dhakatribune.com/magazine/arts-letters/2018/07/14/bringing-the-world-into-the-classroom-through-world-literature> (Accessed: 29 August 2020).

There Was a Bridge in Tekka (2020). Available at: <https://www.wordswithoutborders.org/article/october-2016-multilingual-singapore-there-was-a-bridge-in-tekka-latha> (Accessed: 29 August 2020).

The Un-God (2020). Available at: <https://www.poetrytranslation.org/poems/the-un-god/notes> (Accessed: 5 September 2020).

Latha (2020). Available at: <https://www.poetrytranslation.org/poets/Latha> (Accessed: 5 September 2020).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Investigate how literary traditions are interconnected, revealing shared themes across diverse cultures, fostering mutual understanding and respect through comparative literature.
Address the complexities introduced by globalization, including issues of translation and cultural representation that can obscure authentic voices in world literature.
Encourage the inclusion of diverse voices and perspectives in literary studies, ensuring representation and fostering a richer understanding of global narratives.
